paragraphs.body: My name is Monique Harris, and I am a current freshman at the University of Pittsburgh. You probably don’t have a singular clue on who I am, just know that your letter, “Son of Mr. Green Jeans, an Essay on Fatherhood,” touched my heart. In this essay I noticed you have multiple small summaries about topics relating to fatherhood, while you discuss your own childhood, and paternal relations in your family.

I want to highlight how effectively you managed to use different summaries to bring the overall topic together. One of my particularly favorite small summaries consisted of the passage titled, “Inheritance.” Here you talked about fatherly relationships, and how your father grew up. I was drawn specifically to you mentioning you wanted actor Hugh Beaumont to be your father instead, as the pervious passage mentioned how he despised playing a fatherly role on tv. The stark contrast interested me, as it was a coin flip situation- two sides, one where you both will never truly know how the other feels, as you both are living two different lives. Another thing I want to highlight is your use of animal and nature facts, as it makes me draw the conclusion that paternal instinct is interspecies, and that anyone can be a good father if they have the instinct and put in the effort towards it.

Similar to you, I grew up in a family where bonds between fathers and sons weren't as strong as they could’ve been. As an only child, and daughter to my own father, everything hit me twice as hard when I realized what horrible and abusive actions my father had done. How was it that although he- so violent, was my dad? I didn’t understand that much until I grew older. I used to pour myself into the books I read, praying that one of the fathers in the perfect adventure stories would come out of the printed pages and take me into the story itself, adopting me. Overall, to the best of my ability, I can understand how you felt.

annotations.text: As an only child, and daughter to my own father, everything hit me twice as hard when I realized what horrible and abusive actions my father had done. How was it that although he- so violent, was my dad? I didn’t understand that much until I grew older.
annotations.comment: Thank you for opening up about these very personal issues.  This does make me curious about your own father's upbringing--do you suppose he had a similarly difficult relationship with his own parents? I am also always curious if someone like that would also benefit from reading an essay like Moore's.

paragraphs.body: While reading your essay, I couldn’t help but wonder why you added certain passages. For example, can we highlight the small passage, “Green Genes?” I wonder why you added that, as although it has to do with Ireland's forefathers, it’s regarding a group of people, not a family specifically. Unless you were trying to allude to the people- since they have similar genetic backgrounds, were a family in a greater sense? It makes me think that you’re trying to push past the normal ideas one would consider when thinking of the word ‘father’. I also wonder in the passage titled, “Natural Selection”, why you were so deadset on your wife suggesting kids was a bad idea. I do apologize if it’s too personal, I didn’t want to pry, but due to your past childhood do you think that you’d do a bad job of raising children? Sometimes I worry about myself, although I’m young with many more years ahead of me to learn and make mistakes. What if I make my kids feel the same way my father made me feel? Although those bits were a bit confusing, I’m actually glad you added them. It makes me wonder, and a healthy dosage of wondering is healthy for the soul! I just want to say, thank you for letting me read a fraction of your mind on a page. It makes me happy to know I’m truly not alone in my thoughts. I hope to hear from you soon, if you do get this. Thank you very much Mr. Moore. Best Wishes, Monique Harris

annotations.text: I didn’t want to pry, but due to your past childhood do you think that you’d do a bad job of raising children? Sometimes I worry about myself, although I’m young with many more years ahead of me to learn and make mistakes.
annotations.comment: You're doing a great job of very thoughtfully engaging this text!

annotations.comment: You're doing some excellent work in this letter of engaging a piece of writing through your own experiences and observations. I know it can be very intimidating for anyone to open up about themselves, and you're very bravely sharing your own relationships. I'm looking forward to seeing what you do next.
